question: Which of the following best describes how information systems create business value?
options: By replacing all human workers with automated processes
Through operational efficiency, better decisions, and competitive advantage
By reducing the need for organizational strategy entirely
Through eliminating all data entry and manual record-keeping
explanation: IS creates value through three mechanisms: operational efficiency (same work faster/cheaper), decision quality (better information leads to better choices), and competitive advantage (unique capabilities rivals can't replicate). IS does not eliminate humans, replace strategy, or fully eliminate data entry — it augments organizational capability.

question: A coffee chain uses IS to track which menu items sell best by location and time of day, adjusting staffing accordingly. This primarily illustrates IS creating value through:
options: Competitive advantage through unique technology capabilities
Operational efficiency by matching labor supply to demand
Strategic differentiation from rival coffee chains
Executive decision support for portfolio-level strategy
explanation: Matching staffing to demand patterns is operational efficiency — doing the same work (serving customers) at lower cost (fewer idle staff hours). This is valuable but not a competitive advantage since rivals can adopt the same approach. Competitive advantage requires capabilities that are harder to replicate.

question: What distinguishes IS-enabled competitive advantage from IS-enabled operational efficiency?
options: Competitive advantage is cheaper to build than operational efficiency
Operational efficiency improves internal performance; competitive advantage creates capabilities rivals struggle to match
Competitive advantage only applies to technology companies
Operational efficiency requires custom software; competitive advantage uses off-the-shelf tools
explanation: Operational efficiency makes you better at what you already do — faster, cheaper, fewer errors. Competitive advantage changes what you can do relative to rivals. Every competitor can implement scheduling software (efficiency); not every competitor can replicate Amazon's fulfillment network built over 20 years of IS investment (advantage).

question: Zara's IS connects store sales data directly to design teams, enabling a two-week design-to-shelf cycle versus the industry's six months. Why is this a competitive advantage rather than just operational efficiency?
options: Because Zara spent more money on technology than competitors
Because the capability requires simultaneous IS, supplier, and process changes that rivals cannot quickly replicate
Because Zara's software is proprietary and legally protected
Because operational efficiency and competitive advantage are the same thing
explanation: Zara's speed advantage is durable because replicating it requires changing technology, supplier relationships, organizational processes, and culture simultaneously — not just buying the same software. This is why IS-enabled competitive advantages built into business models are hard to copy. Patent protection and spending levels are not the source of the advantage.

question: A manufacturer implements IS that reduces invoice processing time from 5 days to 4 hours. All major competitors implement the same system within 18 months. What happened to the competitive value of this IS?
options: The competitive value increased as more companies validated the approach
The IS became an operational necessity rather than a competitive differentiator
The IS lost all business value once competitors adopted it
The manufacturer should have kept the system secret to maintain advantage
explanation: When IS capabilities spread across an industry, they shift from competitive advantage to table stakes — you need them to compete, but they no longer differentiate. Faster invoice processing is now an operational baseline, not an edge. The IS still has operational value (efficiency) but no longer has competitive value (differentiation). This is a normal IS lifecycle pattern.

question: Which of the following IS investments is most likely to produce sustained competitive advantage?
options: Upgrading to the latest version of Microsoft Office across the company
Implementing the same ERP system used by 80% of industry peers
Building a proprietary customer data platform that learns from 10 years of purchase history
Installing faster Wi-Fi in all company locations
explanation: Sustained competitive advantage requires IS capabilities that are rare, valuable, and hard to imitate. A proprietary platform built on 10 years of unique customer data cannot be quickly replicated — the data itself is the moat. Generic software upgrades, industry-standard ERP, and infrastructure improvements are available to all competitors equally.

question: A hospital invests $12M in a new IS but patient outcomes don't improve. Which is the most likely explanation?
options: IS investments never improve outcomes in healthcare settings
The IS was technically functional but not aligned with clinical decision processes
The hardware was insufficient for the hospital's transaction volume
The IS vendor provided inadequate post-implementation support
explanation: IS investment without business alignment is a common failure pattern — the system works technically but doesn't change what matters. If clinicians don't use new decision support tools in their actual workflows, or if the IS measures the wrong things, outcomes don't improve regardless of spending. Misalignment between IS capability and organizational need is the most frequent root cause of failed IS investments.

question: Netflix spends heavily on IS for content recommendation and production prediction. A competitor builds the same recommendation algorithm in 6 months. Why might Netflix's IS advantage persist anyway?
options: Netflix holds patents preventing competitors from using recommendation systems
Netflix's algorithm is trained on years of unique viewing data the competitor lacks
Streaming regulations prevent new entrants from using recommendation technology
Netflix's IS advantage comes from hardware speed that cannot be replicated
explanation: The algorithm is replicable; the training data is not. Netflix's 15+ years of global viewing behavior at scale cannot be recreated by a new entrant — the data is the actual competitive asset, not the algorithmic approach. This illustrates why IS advantages based on data accumulation are more durable than those based on software architecture alone.

question: Which of the following best explains why two companies using identical IS can achieve very different business outcomes?
options: One company paid more for implementation and therefore got better results
IS value depends on how well systems align with strategy, people, and processes — not just which tools are used
Identical systems always produce identical outcomes given the same industry
Hardware differences between the two companies explain the outcome gap
explanation: IS value is not intrinsic to the technology — it emerges from alignment between the system and the organization's strategy, people, and processes. The same CRM can produce industry-leading customer retention at one company and near-zero adoption at another. IS is a sociotechnical system: technology plus organizational context determines outcomes.

question: A retailer's IS investment produces a 12% reduction in inventory carrying costs. An analyst argues this has no strategic value. When would this argument be correct?
options: When the retailer is a market leader with no cost pressure
When every competitor achieves the same reduction using the same available tools
When the IS was implemented more than three years ago
When the cost reduction affects warehouse operations rather than customer-facing systems
explanation: Efficiency gains have no strategic value when they are equally accessible to all competitors — they reduce costs industry-wide without creating relative advantage. If every retailer can achieve 12% inventory reduction using the same commercially available IS, no single retailer gains a competitive edge. Strategic value requires asymmetric capability.

question: Porter's value chain framework suggests IS can create value at every activity. Which IS application creates value in the 'outbound logistics' activity specifically?
options: An HR system managing employee performance reviews
A CRM system tracking post-sale customer interactions
A route optimization system reducing delivery time and cost
A procurement system automating supplier purchase orders
explanation: Outbound logistics covers getting finished products to customers — shipping, delivery, distribution. Route optimization IS directly improves this activity. HR systems affect support activities, CRM addresses sales and service, and procurement IS improves inbound logistics. Matching IS applications to value chain activities is a core IS strategy skill.

question: A firm's IS generates abundant data but managers consistently make poor decisions. What is the most accurate diagnosis?
options: The IS is producing data but not actionable information aligned with decisions
The managers need to be replaced with more data-literate staff
The IS hardware is generating corrupted data outputs
More data always leads to better decisions given sufficient volume
explanation: Data abundance without decision alignment is a common IS failure. If the IS produces metrics that don't map to the actual decisions managers face, or if outputs require analytical skills managers lack, data volume is irrelevant. IS value comes from information that reduces uncertainty in specific decisions — not from data generation alone.

question: Which of the following IS scenarios creates network effect value — where the system becomes more valuable as more people use it?
options: An accounting system that automates monthly journal entries
A route optimization system that improves delivery scheduling
A professional networking platform where connections increase everyone's access
An inventory system that reduces warehouse carrying costs
explanation: Network effects occur when each additional user increases value for all existing users — LinkedIn becomes more valuable as more professionals join because everyone's network expands. Accounting automation, route optimization, and inventory systems have fixed value per user — they don't become more powerful as adoption grows. Network effects are a powerful IS-enabled competitive mechanism.

question: A company calculates its IS ROI by measuring only cost savings. What important source of IS value does this approach miss?
options: Hardware depreciation schedules and maintenance contracts
Revenue growth, decision quality improvements, and strategic positioning
Employee satisfaction scores from IS usability surveys
Vendor support quality and implementation timelines
explanation: Cost savings capture only the efficiency dimension of IS value. Revenue growth enabled by better customer IS, improved decisions from better analytics, and strategic positioning from unique IS capabilities are often larger sources of value than cost reduction — but harder to measure. IS ROI calculations that ignore the top line systematically undervalue strategic IS investments.

question: Which condition is necessary for IS to create sustained competitive advantage according to the resource-based view?
options: The IS must be purchased from a market-leading vendor
The IS capability must be valuable, rare, and difficult for competitors to imitate
The IS must process more transactions per second than competitor systems
The IS must have been implemented before competitors entered the market
explanation: The resource-based view holds that sustainable competitive advantage requires resources that are valuable (they help), rare (competitors lack them), and inimitable (hard to copy). IS capabilities meeting all three criteria — like Amazon's logistics network or Google's search algorithm — produce durable advantage. Vendor brand, transaction speed, and first-mover timing alone don't satisfy these conditions.
